medications.indication_rationale: Symptomatic relief of mild odynophagia; source advises 1000mg up to four times daily.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 1000mg orally up to four times daily as needed (max 4g/day) (as per source note)

medications.indication_rationale: Acute laryngitis is viral; antibiotics are ineffective and inappropriate.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Rest the voice — avoid talking and especially avoid whispering; write things down.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Whispering strains the vocal folds more than soft normal voice; true rest speeds recovery.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Hydrate well, use a cool-mist humidifier, and avoid smoke.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Humidification and hydration reduce mucosal drying and irritation.

patient_education_points.point: This is viral laryngitis — the voice box is swollen from a virus. Antibiotics won't help; the voice returns as the swelling settles, usually within 1–2 weeks.
Rest your voice and don't whisper — whispering actually strains the cords more than talking softly. Write notes instead.
Go to the ED or call 000 if you develop noisy/whistling breathing, trouble breathing, can't swallow your saliva, start drooling, or the throat pain suddenly gets much worse.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Voice recovery; refer for laryngoscopy if hoarseness persists beyond 2–3 weeks.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Any breathing difficulty, stridor, drooling or inability to swallow saliva — call 000 immediately.

must_include_items: Airway safety-net (stridor, breathing difficulty, drooling, can't swallow saliva → 000/ED)
Voice rest with explicit advice not to whisper
Reassurance that it is viral and self-limiting; no antibiotics
acceptable_alternatives: Simple analgesia (paracetamol or NSAID) acceptable for odynophagia
GP review at 7–10 days or laryngoscopy referral at >2–3 weeks both acceptable
errors_of_omission: Failing to exclude epiglottitis/tracheitis before reassurance
errors_of_commission: Sending an uncomplicated viral laryngitis with a negative airway screen to ED by ambulance
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Pass requires an explicit airway safety-net, voice-rest advice and no antibiotics. Missing the airway safety-net in an acute aphonia is a critical failure.
